coredata.dc:title: Rapid additive manufacturing of all-carbon, all-dielectric metastructures
coredata.dc:creator: Jaiswal, Arun
Rani, Sweta
Singh, Gaurav Pratap
Archana, Thachanamoorthy
Hassan, Mahbub
Nasrin, Aklima
Gomes, Vincent G.
Saxena, Sumit
Shukla, Shobha
coredata.dc:description: Metamaterials have been explored to achieve extraordinary response to incident radiations, not accessible to conventional materials. Metamaterial attributes are derived from spatially arranged sub-wavelength resolved metaelements. Additive manufacturing technologies offer capabilities to fabricate complex metastructures with novel remarkable responses. Two-photon polymerization (TPP) based additive manufacturing has gained popularity due to its exceptional precision and minimal processing stages. However, the major bottleneck in the development of TPP based fabrication is its dependence on engineered/proprietary/expensive two-photon active materials for initiation of polymerization process. Here, we propose an acrylate and carbon dot composite resin for additive manufacturing of all-carbon, all-dielectric metamaterials. Two-photon irradiation induces photo-induced electron transfer from the surface states of carbon quantum dots, which in turn initiates the TPP. The composite resin is devoid of any other two-photon dye/initiator. The effect of carbon dot concentration on fabrication process parameters and fabrication resolution have been systematically investigated. Several linear/complex planar and 3D structures have been fabricated with high replication fidelity. As a proof-of-concept, the composite resinhas been used for the realization of resonant and chiral metamaterials. Furthermore, theoretical calculations are also performed to evaluate the optical activity of these metastructures. Our results demonstrate steps towards realizing all-dielectric, all-carbon based metamaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metamaterials
Additive manufacturing
Two-photon polymerization
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of ACCR and fabricated structures

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: India Ministry of Science & Technology Department of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge financial support from the Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, through grant no. DST/INT/AUS/P-82/2022(G), grant no. DST/TM/ WTI/WIC/2K17/100(C), Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) grant no. EMR/2017/005144 and Department of Science and Technology, Solar Energy Research Initiative (SERI), Government of India, with a grant via sanction order no. DST/TM/SERI/2k10/12/(G) for this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-c). Designs of IITB logo, L-type structure and a cut-pyramid structure, (d). Optical microscope image of IITB logo, fabricated over an area of 50 μm × 50 μm, (e). SEM image of an array of L-type structures, (f). SEM image showing the top-view of an array of cut pyramid structures; inset shows a single element of the array, (g,). A digital image of a quick-read (QR) code of our lab webpage (https://homepages.iitb.ac.in/∼sshukla/), (h). Optical microscope image of micro-replica of (g), fabricated over an area of 50 μm × 50 μm, (i). Optical microscope image of two identical replicas of QR code fabricated side by side, and (j). Image of a read-out obtained from the image of micro-QR code, via a google lens scanner. Scale bars in h &i are 25 μm.

SEM images of (a). Suspended wire structures supported by blocks at edges, (b,c). Zoomed view of (a), revealing the fine structures and resolution of a nanowire fabricated in ACCR10. A lateral resolution of ∼320 nm was achieved for nanowires fabricated at 40 mW at a writing speed of 25 μm/s in ACCR 10 and (d). Power vs linewidth plot ACCR1 to ACCR10. Scan speed was kept constantfor all the experiments.

(a-g). Optical microscope images of gratings fabricated in ACCR0.1 to ACCR20. All the fabrication parameters were kept constant, exposure power 100 mW, scan speed 500 μm/s, (h). Fluorescent images of lines with different CQD loading numbers indicate CQD loading (wt%). Images were captured under a UV excitation (360 nm to 380 nm) with average intensity of 3 mW and (i). Average emission intensities obtained from fluorescent images of line elements containing different (wt%) of carbon quantum dots loading. Numbers indicated in the images(h) and (i), correspond to wt% of CQD loaded in the resin. Scale bars in (a-h) are 5 µm.

(a). SEM image of an array of pyramids showing the top view, (b-d) Zoomed views of (a), revealing the fine details of the structure, (e). Tilted view captured at 45ο, showing the high aspect ratio of the fabricated structure and (f, g). The top view and tilted view of the structure, generated by 3D stacking of the fluorescent images. Scale bars in the images represented in (a-e) are 10 μm.

(a-c). SEM images of double U-bar shaped metastructures captured at various magnification, (d). A 3D model of the structure, and the theoretical reflection response of the structure measured under (e). RCP and (f). LCP excitations.

head.ce:title: Rapid additive manufacturing of all-carbon, all-dielectric metastructures

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanostructures Engineering and Modeling Laboratory, Department of Metallurgical Engineering and Materials Science, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metamaterials have been explored to achieve extraordinary response to incident radiations, not accessible to conventional materials. Metamaterial attributes are derived from spatially arranged sub-wavelength resolved metaelements. Additive manufacturing technologies offer capabilities to fabricate complex metastructures with novel remarkable responses. Two-photon polymerization (TPP) based additive manufacturing has gained popularity due to its exceptional precision and minimal processing stages. However, the major bottleneck in the development of TPP based fabrication is its dependence on engineered/proprietary/expensive two-photon active materials for initiation of polymerization process. Here, we propose an acrylate and carbon dot composite resin for additive manufacturing of all-carbon, all-dielectric metamaterials. Two-photon irradiation induces photo-induced electron transfer from the surface states of carbon quantum dots, which in turn initiates the TPP. The composite resin is devoid of any other two-photon dye/initiator. The effect of carbon dot concentration on fabrication process parameters and fabrication resolution have been systematically investigated. Several linear/complex planar and 3D structures have been fabricated with high replication fidelity. As a proof-of-concept, the composite resinhas been used for the realization of resonant and chiral metamaterials. Furthermore, theoretical calculations are also performed to evaluate the optical activity of these metastructures. Our results demonstrate steps towards realizing all-dielectric, all-carbon based metamaterials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Structurally engineered sub-wavelength sized elements; “meta-elements”, arranged in a spatially complex architecture has been used for dynamic/static steering of incident radiations. These structures exhibit unusual responses, otherwise not exhibited by conventional materials. Novel phenomena, such as, inverse Doppler effect [1], negative refractive index [2], cloaking [3], hyperbolic dispersion [4], perfect absorbtion [5], superlensing [6], high permitivitty [7] etc., have been realized using metamaterials (MMs).

Early theoretical prediction of MMs was presented in the pioneering work of Veselago et al. [8]. Experimental realization of metamaterials was demonstrated by several other groups in the subsequent years [9–13]. Most of the MMs reported in literature are derived from either metallic or metal-dielectric metaelements [14–17]. However, these structures are associated with inherent drawbacks, such as, narrow bandwidth and ohmic losses [18,19]. Further, the realization of these MMs relied on conventional additive [20] and subtractive manufacturing [21] technologies. Techniques, such as, electron beam lithography and focused ion beam lithography are unsuitable for fabrication of 3D geometries, where structurally complex metaelements are involved and hence are limited to fabrication of planar geometries. Due to these limitations, there have been a persistent interest towards developing all-dielectric MMs (ADMMs). Several ADMMs have been developed using dielectrics, polymers and composites. Amongst these, polymer based MMs have wide potential due to high degree of freedom in the design of metaelements using vat polymerization techniques such as daylight polymer printing [22], digital light processing [23], stereolithography [24] and two-photon lithography [25] (TPL). Although vat polymerization based additive manufacturing technologies have been progressing rapidly, fabrication of complex nano-scale structures over large area is still a challenging proposition [26]. For instance, daylight polymer printing allows fabrication with printing rates as high as∼100 mm/h but offer minimal features of ∼20 μm3 [27,28]. On the other hand, TPL has shown to be promising in terms of precision, and achieving resolution up to ∼80 nm [29,30]. Additionally, TPL can be used to fabricate structures using pure polymeric as well as doped/composite resins [31–40]; thus, providing a possibility to alter the material composition of the metaelements and hence the behavior and response of MMs. However, conventional TPL suffers from low throughput due to single point processing at a time and limited volumes offered by high numerical aperture focusing optics. A combination of optical engineering and material science is required for enhancement of TPL printing rates and throughput. Various formulation of resists have been explored for achieving high two-photon polymerization (TPP) sensitivity [41,42] and beam engineering and have been implemented towards parallelization/multi-focus printing to increase the throughput of TPL [43,44]. Exploiting these capabilities of TPL, the researchers have developed a variety of MMs including phase programmable microdomes [45], 4D thermomechanical metamaterials [46], microacoustic metagratings [47], chiral metamaterial-based microstepper [48] and 3D auxetic metamaterials [49]. Although, TPL have been pivotal in realization of wide variety of MMs, these MMs were based on the commercial resins with fixed material composition, wherein MM responses were realized by systematic implementation of hierarchy and/or size effects. Few reports have also highlighted development of carbon-based ADMMs as metasurfaces, antennas, metamaterial absorbers etc., majorly using graphene, laser-induced graphene, carbon nanotubes and other functional composites based on carbon nanomaterials [50]. Recently, pyrolysis of photolithographically produced micro-patterns was demonstrated for the generation of carbonized microstructures [51]. However, the fabrication either relies on serial processing or is limited to planar geometries. To exploit the 3D attributes, and to tap into the true potential of material domain, development of carbon-based two-photon active resins allowing control over the material composition are much required. In this quest, we recently reported development of acrylate and carbon quantum dot composite resin (ACCR) [52–54], which exhibited high TPP sensitivity and demonstrated fabrication speed as high as 100 mm/s. These results suggest the use of high TPP sensitivity of ACCR, along with the high-speed writing of nano-scaled structures for rapid additive manufacturing of carbon-based ADMMs. Additionally, ACCR allows the tuning of material composition, enabling the futuristic implications in the materials domain.

In this work, we report the fabrication of all-carbon, all-dielectric MMs via TPL of ACCR. This report includes systematic experimental investigations of the impact of CDs loading (1–10 wt%) on the fabrication resolution and emissive traits of the fabricated structure. Initially, we have optimized the material composition and process parameters, and evaluated the printing performance of the resin in terms of various quantitative (speed and resolution) and qualitative parameters (repeatability, scalability, and replication fidelity). We have also conducted systematic investigations of optical properties (fluorescence emission) of the polymerized structures for resin containing 0 wt% to - 20 wt% of CDs. Further, we have demonstrated the potential of the resin to generate carbon-based metastructures consisting double U and y shaped metaelements. We have also performed the systematic theoretical investigation of metastructure response including the transmission and reflection coefficients, the variation of polarization angle vs frequency and circular dichroism of the proposed metastructure. Additionally, the fabrication of high aspect ratio 3D structures including pyramid and microcones have also been demonstrated. The work offers additional degree of freedom in material design by allowing the variation of resin composition, and the doping concentration of the nanomaterials defines the mechanical and optical properties of the polymerized structures [55].

Systematic studies were performed to evaluate the printing performance and the effect of CQD loading in ACCR. Among all the ACCRx (where x=0–20 denotes wt% CQDs) formulations used, ACCR20 offered lowest writing threshold, broadest working window and highest fluorescent emission. Fortunately, large amount of CQDs is not required to attain optimum TPP sensitivity and fabrication with sub-wavelength resolved features. To evaluate the dependence of CQD loading on the process parameters, fabrication resolution and printing performances, two sets of experiments were performed. Firstly, several microreplicas of 2D and 3D models were fabricated in ACCR1. Secondly, a series of suspended wire structures and gratings were fabricated in ACCR1 to ACCR20 to evaluate the writing threshold and emissive traits of the nanowires, respectively.

Structures ranging from complex 2D, quasi 3D and real 3D, were fabricated with ACCR1, to demonstrate the capability of fabrication of any arbitrary structure via TPL. An image of Indian Institute of Technology Bombay (IITB) logo, a quasi-3D L-type structure and 3D model of cut pyramid structures are shown in Fig. 1(a-c), and images of their microreplicas are shown in Fig. 1(d-f). The IITB logo is a complex structure with inscriptions in English as well as Hindi languages. Additionally, it contains several curved features. The structure was fabricated over an area of 50 μm × 50 μm with good replication fidelity as seen in the optical microscope image of the fabricated logo. This established theability to write an arbitrarily complex 2D structure. Further, an array of quasi 3D L-type structure was manufactured over an area of 100 μm × 100 μm. The SEM image of the structure, Fig. 1(e), shows the uniformity over long range order. 3D micropyramids were fabricated with good replication fidelity as shown in Fig. 1(f), where three identical pyramids are shown (zoomed view of a single pyramid is shown in the inset).

Further, a micro QR code for our lab webpage ( https://homepages.iitb.ac.in/∼sshukla/ ) was fabricated over an area of 50 μm × 50μm. The digital image and the optical microscope image of the QR code is shown in Fig. 1(g) & Fig. 1(h), respectively. Two identical micro-QR codes were fabricated, and the structural attributes of these fabricated structures were identical, as shown in Fig. 1(i), indicating the repeatability of the fabrication process. The message embedded in the code was successfully decoded when the optical microscope image was scanned through a google lens scanner. The image of the readout is shown in the Fig. 1(h). A 2D mesh structure with 10 μm periodicity was fabricated over an area of 0.924 cm × 0.924 cm, demonstrating the scalable printing process. The image of the entire structure and SEM image showing the zoomed view of the structure are shown in Figure S1 (a) & Figure S1 (b), respectively. We can, thus conclude that the TPL of ACCR is a robust nanoadditive manufacturing process with high replication fidelity, repeatability, and scalability.

The effect of CQD loading on the process parameters (writing threshold, scan speed) and fabrication resolution were studied extensively for ACCR1 to ACCR10. A set of suspended wire structures were fabricated for each resin. The laser power input to the focusing objective was varied from 20 mW to 200 mW, and scan speed was kept constant at 25 μm/s for all the experiments. The SEM image of the suspended wire structure is shown in Fig. 2(a), and zoomed views shown in Fig. 2(b c) display a section of the suspended wires and a single nanowire, respectively. SEM images of the suspended wire structures, fabricated for linewidth measurements are shown in Figure S2 & S3. A lateral resolution of ∼320 nm was obtained for ACCR10, at writing threshold of 40 mW. A reduction in the lateral resolution was observed with increase in laser power for all resins as shown in power vs linewidth plot in Fig. 2(d). The respective values of lateral resolution are tabulated in Table T1 of the supplementary information. It was observed that the writing threshold increases with decrease in CQD loading in the resin. This behavior can be attributed to an increase in the number of available absorbers per unit volume per unit time with increased CQD loading. The trends of power vs linewidth and variation of writing threshold with varying initiator are in line with reports in the literature [56–58].

To further investigate the impact of CQD loading on the fluorescent emission of the polymerized structures, a set of microgratings were fabricated in ACCR0.5 to ACCR20 and characterized under a fluorescent microscope. The corresponding optical microscope images of the microgratings are shown in Fig. 3 (a-f). Fabrication of all the structures were conducted under identical exposure conditions: 100 mW average power and scan speed of 500 μm/s. It can be noticed that the lateral resolutions of the gratings fabricated in ACCR20 to ACCR2.5 were similar, whereas there was a prominent change in the lateral linewidth of the gratings fabricated in ACCR1 and ACCR0.5. This observation can be attributed to power vs linewidth trend, where the variations in the lateral resolutions are much pronounced while working close to the writing threshold; whereas the curve flattens at higher exposures [47]. The fluorescent images and the corresponding average emission intensities of the line elements are shown in Fig. 3 (g & h), respectively. The emission intensities of the polymerized structures increased with increased CQD loading. Similar trend was observed for the unpolymerized resin as shown in Figure S4. Thus, the ACCR resin is suitable for generating highly fluorescent microstructures and the emission can be utilized for high contrast fluorescent imaging.

To exploit the emissive properties of the fabricated structure towards non-invasive 3D imaging, an array of 3D pyramid structure was fabricated with ACCR1 and characterized using fluorescent imaging. The base of each pyramid measured 28 μm × 28 μm, height of 40 μm with a top of 7 μm × 7 μm. The SEM images of the top view of the pyramid structures and their fine details can be seen in Fig. 4(a-d). Fig. 4(e) reveals the high aspect ratio of the pyramids. The 3D model of the pyramid was generated via stacking of the fluorescent images acquired at various depths of the structure. The top view and the tilted view of the reconstructed models are shown in Fig. 4(f & g), respectively. The tilted view of micropyramids was acquired using SEM and the 3D reconstructed model, exhibiting a good co-relation, and revealing high contrast of fluorescent imaging. A series of micropyramids with varied aspect ratios and high aspect ratio microneedles were fabricated and investigated via SEM and fluorescent imaging. The corresponding images are shown in Figures S5, S6 and Figures S7, S8 respectively.

In order to demonstrate the applications of ACCR for fabrication of the metamaterial, the scope of use of resin was furthered to fabricate metastructures by exploiting the sub-micron accuracy of the printing process. SEM images of an array of double U shaped metaelements and their zoomed views are shown in Fig. 5(a-c). Theoretical calculations were performed to evaluate the optical response of the metastructures, using full-wave electromagnetic (FEM) analysis. The three-dimensional model of the double U-shaped metastructure is shown in Fig. 5(d), the transmission and reflection coefficients (S21 and S11), for right-handed circular polarized (RCP) and left-handed circular polarized (LCP), excitations were evaluated. The reflection and transmission response of the metastructure under RCP and LCP excitations are shown in Fig. 5(e &f), and Figure S9, respectively. Multiple sharp peaks were observed in the transmission and reflection response of the metaelement. For reflection response, peaks centred around 45 THz, 130 THz and 185 THz were observed. The polarization angle vs frequency plot for RCP and LCP excitation is shown in Figure S10 (a & b) respectively. The structure exhibits circular dichroism (CD) at ∼170 THz as shown in Figure S10 (c). Furthermore, a metastructure consisting of a y-shaped metaelement was also fabricated and characterized. Their SEM images, exhibit precise control of fabrication resolution over the subwavelength regime, as shown in Figure S11 (a-c). The computational model of a single y-shaped metaelement, and their reflection responses are shown in Figure S11 (d-f). The plot of reflection coefficients, under RCP and LCP excitation demonstrates a sharp dip at ∼100 THz. The theoretical results indicate the potential application of both these metastructures in realization of frequency selective elements of nanophotonics devices. Excitonic coupling between the polymer chains and the quantum dots are known to facilitate enhanced optical response in composites of polymer and quantum dots [59]. The CQDs are homogenously distributed within the polymerized structures, as revealed by the fluorescent images of the split-ring resonator and the L-type structure shown in Figure S12. Similar behaviour is expected for an arbitrary geometry with metamaterial structures fabricated using ACCR. Thus, we expect that the CQDs will acquire the geometry of the metastructure itself. The CQDs dispersed in metamaterial geometry are expected to offer enhanced metamaterial response, and the systematic investigation of their cumulative responses can offer new insights towards development of all-carbon based dielectric metamaterials. Investigation and analysis of these responses is intriguing and will be addressed in future studies.

In the presented work, we have utilized the bimodal behaviour of the CQDs. By strategic tailoring of the CQD surface functionalities, novel tuneable and 4D metamaterials with non-toxic biocompatible characteristics are envisioned. Photo-induced electron transfer from two-photon active CQDs have been harnessed for rapid additive manufacturing of nanostructures with high replication fidelity, repeatability, and scalability. The effect of CQD loading on process operating parameters, fabrication resolution and emissive behavior of resin as well as polymerized structures have been systematically investigated to attain precise control over nanostructuring. A lateral resolution of ∼320 nm was achieved for ACCR10. Various 2D and 3D nanostructures ranging from nanowires to high aspect ratio pyramids and microneedles have been produced to demonstrate the range of applicability. The emissive characteristics of the polymerized structures were employed for fluorescent imaging with high contrast, and subsequently applied for 3D reconstruction of fabricated structures via non-invasive, non-destructive approach. Further, a double-U shaped metastructures was fabricated and characterized, demonstrating the ability to generate high quality metaelements and repeatability of structures over long range order. The experimental investigation of the dependence of the emission intensity of the polymerized structures on the CQD loading, provide useful insights on the ability to control the properties of the final polymerized structures by varying the resin composition, hence allowing the ability to tap in the material domain to attain tailored properties. The capability of generating metaelements with intricate geometries is expected to instigate novel implications of all-dielectric metamaterials in realization of current and next generation functional elements for photonic technologies.

Two-photon active CQDs were prepared by one-pot, facile hydrothermal synthesis using curcumin precursor [60]. Two monomers from Sartomer; SR368-Tris (2-hydroxyethyl) isocyanurate acrylate and the SR499-ethoxylated (6) trimethylolpropane triacrylate (SR499) were mixed in equal weight proportions to obtain Sartomer resin (SR). To obtain ACCR, the powdered CQDs were added and subsequently mixed in SR. CQD loading was varied from 0.1 wt% to 20 wt% and the samples were named ACCRx; x represents the amount of CQD loading in wt%. The formulated resin was kept in a vacuum desiccator and stored in the dark for further use. It is worth noting that, no prominent change was observed in the optical properties of the resin and two-photon processability.

A uniform film of ACCR was prepared by spin coating the resin on a cleaned glass coverslip. The film was then irradiated with 800 nm, 140 fs mode-locked pulses of Coherent Chameleon Ultra I, operating at a pulse repetition rate of 80 MHz, tightly focused onto the resin using a 100X, 0.9 NA objective lens assembly. The exposed area underwent photo-polymerization, generating a polymerized voxel. The structures were generated by 3D translation of a nanoprecision stage in a predefined trajectory. The unexposed regions of the film were washed away by rinsing the entire film in dimethylformamide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of ACCR and fabricated structures
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning electron microcope (SEM, Auriga Zeiss dual beam FEG-SEM), confocal optical microscope (Olympus IX81) and confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Carl Zeiss, LSM 780) were extensively used for characterization of the fabricated structures and resins.

Mahbub Hassan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Thachanamoorthy Archana: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Vincent G. Gomes: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources. Aklima Nasrin: Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Sumit Saxena: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Shobha Shukla: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Arun Jaiswal: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Gaurav Pratap Singh: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Sweta Rani: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge financial support from the Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, through grant no. DST/INT/AUS/P-82/2022(G), grant no. DST/TM/ WTI/WIC/2K17/100(C), Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) grant no. EMR/2017/005144 and Department of Science and Technology, Solar Energy Research Initiative (SERI), Government of India, with a grant via sanction order no. DST/TM/SERI/2k10/12/(G) for this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.addma.2024.104091.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Observation of the inverse doppler effect

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Seddon, N.; Bearpark, T. Observation of the Inverse Doppler Effect. Science, 2003, 302 (5650), 1537–1540.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Composite medium with simultaneously negative permeability and permittivity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Smith, D.R.; Padilla, W.J.; Vier, D.C.; Nemat-Nasser, S.C.; Schultz, S. Composite Medium with Simultaneously Negative Permeability and Permittivity. Phys. Rev. Lett., 2000, 84 (18), 4184–4187.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metamaterial electromagnetic cloak at microwave frequencies

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sub-Diffraction-Limited Optical Imaging with a Silver Superlens

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave metamaterials made by fused deposition 3D printing of a highly conductive copper-based filament

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xie, Y.; Ye, S.; Reyes, C.; Sithikong, P.; Popa, B.-I.; Wiley, B.J.; Cummer, S.A. Microwave Metamaterials Made by Fused Deposition 3D Printing of a Highly Conductive Copper-Based Filament. Appl. Phys. Lett., 2017, 110 (18), 181903.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The Electrodynamics of substances with simultaneously negative values of ε and μ

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Veselago, V.G. The Electrodynamics of Substances with Simultaneously Negative Values of ε and μ. Sov. Phys. Uspekhi, 1968, 10 (4), 509.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Experimental verification of a negative index of refraction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shelby, R.A.; Smith, D.R.; Schultz, S. Experimental Verification of a Negative Index of Refraction. Science, 2001, 292 (5514), 77–79.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metamaterial electromagnetic cloak at microwave frequencies
